text: The surgeon retracts the uterus anteriorly to improve visualization of the surgical field. The retroperitoneum is opened to the right of the large pelvic mass, extending laterally. Firefly technology is utilized to visually identify the ureter during the procedure.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection to expose the right side of the large pelvic mass, confirming the ureteral location using Firefly technology. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the mass, staying close to the capsule.
The surgeon displaces the sigmoid colon anteriorly and laterally, revealing the left ureter along the left pelvic sidewall, consistent with preoperative imaging.
The surgeon frees the superior aspect of the pelvic mass circumferentially, allowing for increased mobility as the dissection progresses distally.

The surgeon performs lateral and interior dissection down to the pelvic floor and the distal extent of the large pelvic mass. A digital rectal exam confirms the location. The posterior dissection is challenging due to the mass's size and bulkiness.

The surgeon encounters significant adhesions posteriorly, likely due to a preoperative biopsy. After reaching the extent of the robotic dissection, a fan and steel incision is made to complete the remaining mobilization, allowing for the intact removal of the tumor.
